retrieved_docs.title: Neck Cancer Resection and Dissection  -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the anatomical structures in head and neck cancer dissection. Describe the indications for head and neck cancer surgery. Review the complications of head and neck surgery. Summarize the importance of care coordination among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for patients undergoing head and neck surgery. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Neck Dissection -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the key anatomic relationships in the neck. Review the indications for performing radical neck dissection, modified radical neck dissection, and selective neck dissection. Explain common complications associated with radical neck dissections. Explain the expected clinical outcomes after radical neck dissection, contrasted with modified radical and selective neck dissection. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Neck Dissection -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Head and neck cancers most commonly metastasize initially to the cervical nodal basins. As a result, removing the cervical lymph nodes is an important component of the standard of care for many head and neck cancers. Radical neck dissection refers to removing levels I-V along with the SCM, IJV, and CN XI. The modified radical neck dissection also removes levels I-V but spares at least one non-lymphatic structure (SCM, IJV, or CN XI). Selective neck dissection refers to any procedure which removes one or more levels of the neck based on patterns of cervical metastasis. Finally, an extended neck dissection refers to any neck dissection that removes additional structures of lymph nodes from areas not addressed in radical neck dissection. This activity reviews the indications and procedural steps for radical neck dissection and briefly discusses other variants of neck dissection for head and neck cancer. This activity highlight the role of the healthcare team in managing patients with head and neck cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Neck Cancer Resection and Dissection  -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Head and neck cancer is the sixth most common cancer worldwide. The single most important factor affecting prognosis for squamous cell carcinoma is the status of the cervical lymph nodes. Metastasis to the regional lymph nodes reduces the 5-year survival rate by 50% compared with that of patients with early-stage disease. The American Cancer Society reports that 40% of patients with squamous carcinoma of the oral cavity and pharynx present with regional metastases to the cervical lymph nodes. This activity presents the causes, pathophysiology, indications, contraindications of head and neck cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Neck Cancer Resection and Dissection  -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: 1. Supraomohyoid neck dissection (SOHD): Lymph nodes removed are Levels I–II, with sparing of IJV, SCM, and accessory nerve. SOHD is indicated in the N0 neck for primary SCC or malignant melanoma where the primary site is anterior to the ear or is located in the lower eyelid (but should include parotidectomy for face and forehead/anterior scalp).

retrieved_docs.title: Neck Cancer Resection and Dissection  -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: 2. Selective neck dissection (SND): Removal of lymph nodes in levels Ib–IV, with sparing of IJV, SCM, and SAN. The procedure is indicated in N0 neck for SCC of the lateral tongue, oral cavity, anterior floor of mouth, or for N1 disease in these primary sites. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Neck Cancer Resection and Dissection  -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: 5. Modified radical neck dissection: As described above. This procedure has an indication for thyroid cancer and upper aerodigestive carcinomas with positive lateral neck nodes but limited extracapsular spread. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Neck Cancer Resection and Dissection  -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: There are no absolute contraindications to neck dissection beyond those that make a patient unfit for general anesthesia and resection, with one exception: unresectable disease. Relative contraindications include: [12] [13] Severe cardiopulmonary disease, COPD with poor functional status Preoperative imaging showing deep infiltration of the tumor in the prevertebral space, scalene muscles, levator scapula muscle, phrenic nerve, and brachial plexus are not suitable candidates The primary tumor that is uncontrollable. Distant metastatic disease Tumor encasement of the carotid artery (NOTE: many authors view this as 'unresectable disease,' as resection and grafting does not confer a survival or local control advantage, even if it is technically possible) [14] Absolute contraindication: Fixed neck mass in the deep neck muscles, prevertebral fascia, and/or skull base involvement (unresectable disease)

retrieved_docs.title: Neck Cancer Resection and Dissection  -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: 3. Lateral neck dissection (also technically a selective neck dissection): Removal of lymph nodes from levels II-IV with sparing of IJV, SCM, and accessory nerve. Indications for this procedure are any N-stage neck without significant extracapsular spread for SCC of larynx and hypopharynx, for differentiated thyroid carcinoma, or for melanoma with a positive sentinel lymph node that drains to these nodal basins. Certain primary parotid malignancies also warrant this operation.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Neck Dissection -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: A neck dissection is generally indicated for any clinically positive nodal disease or advanced tumor stage in node-negative disease (T3-T4). Radical neck dissection is reserved only for clinically positive resectable neck disease involving the SCM, IJV, and CN XI.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Neck Dissection -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Selective neck dissection and modified radical neck dissection (MRND) are the gold standard for clinically positive, resectable neck disease. Selective neck dissection is frequently performed for clinically and radiographically node-negative disease with a high T-stage (T3-T4). In patients with clinically negative necks and tumors that place them at high risk of cervical metastasis (greater than 20%), a selective neck dissection is performed of the appropriate nodal basins based on the tumor’s location. [5] F

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2573
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis to head and neck lymph node is classified as a secondary malignancy, indicating that the spread of cancer to these regional lymph nodes represents a secondary tumor rather than a primary disease. Recognizing this mapping is important for accurate staging, prognosis assessment, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2502
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of the neck is classified as a secondary malignancy, reflecting the spread of a primary cancer to regional lymphatic tissue. Recognizing this metastatic involvement as a secondary malignancy is important for accurate staging, prognosis, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2340
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis to head and neck lymph node and metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck are both classified as secondary malignancies, reflecting the spread of a primary cancer to regional lymphatic tissue in the head and neck region. In addition, metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is directly linked to the morphological pattern described by the Arabic term “السرطان النقيلي,” which denotes metastatic cancer. Recognizing these entities as secondary malignancies and understanding their characteristic metastatic morphology are important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and management of patients with head‑and‑neck cancer spread.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head refers to any cancer that has spread to structures within the head region, and within this broader category the specific condition of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head represents a more precise manifestation. Recognizing that metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head is a subset of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head helps clinicians assess the extent of disease spread, determine appropriate staging, and tailor management strategies for patients with head‑region metastases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 1112
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck, Metastatic adenocarcinoma to lymph node of head and neck, and Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck are all classified as secondary malignancies. The broader category نقائل إلى الرقبة encompasses Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck, indicating an inverse hierarchical relationship. In terms of morphological description, Metastatic adenocarcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is associated with the morphology termed سرطانة غدية نقيلية, while Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck corresponds to the morphology described as السرطان النقيلي. These relationships highlight that metastatic involvement of cervical and head‑and‑neck lymph nodes represents secondary cancer spread, with specific histopathologic subtypes identified by both English and Arabic terminology, which is important for accurate diagnosis, coding, and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2395
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to head is a broad category of cancer spread that encompasses more specific manifestations such as metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of head. The latter represents a distinct subset of head metastases, focusing on involvement of the regional lymphatic structures. Because metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of head reflects disease that has spread from a primary site, it is classified under secondary malignancies, the overarching term for cancers that arise as metastases rather than as primary tumors. Recognizing that lymph node involvement in the head is both a specific form of head metastasis and a type of secondary malignancy helps clinicians accurately stage disease, guide treatment planning, and communicate prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ) is clinically associated with head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق), indicating that smoking is a significant risk factor for the development of malignancies in the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, and related structures. Head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق) can manifest as metastasis to head and neck lymph nodes (Metastasis to head and neck lymph node), reflecting a common pattern of regional spread for these tumors. Together, these concepts underscore the importance of tobacco cessation in preventing head and neck cancer and the need for careful evaluation of cervical lymph nodes when diagnosing and staging the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2712
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck, referred to in Arabic as نقائل إلى الرقبة, represents a secondary malignancy in which cancer cells have spread from a primary site to the cervical lymph nodes. Recognizing this condition as a secondary malignancy guides clinicians in staging, prognosis, and therapeutic decisions, emphasizing the importance of identifying metastatic involvement of neck lymph nodes in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 933
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of the thyroid. Thyroid cancer is clinically associated with the procedure استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). Thyroidectomy, whether performed as a partial or complete operation (thyroidectomy; partial or complete), is a targeted procedure for the head and neck structure and serves as the parent procedure for both استئصال الدرقية (full thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). The partial thyroidectomy (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي) frequently co‑occurs with دراق, reflecting a contextual relationship. Understanding these interconnections is essential for planning appropriate surgical management of thyroid cancer within the head and neck region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2185
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic adenocarcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is classified as a secondary malignancy and is linked to the morphological description given by the Arabic term سرطانة غدية نقيلية, which denotes a metastatic adenocarcinoma. Recognizing that this specific head‑and‑neck lymph node involvement represents a secondary cancer and corresponds to the described morphology helps clinicians accurately diagnose and appropriately manage patients presenting with such metastatic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2584
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is classified as a secondary malignancy and is characterized by the morphological pattern described by the Arabic term ال سرطان النقيلي, which denotes metastatic cancer. This relationship highlights that involvement of head and neck lymph nodes by metastatic carcinoma represents a spread of a primary tumor to a distant site, fitting the definition of secondary malignancy and reflecting the specific histopathologic features associated with metastatic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 791
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of the head, metastatic adenocarcinoma to a lymph node of the head and neck, and metastatic carcinoma to a lymph node of the head and neck are all classified as secondary malignancies, reflecting their nature as cancerous lesions that have spread from a primary site to regional lymphatic tissue. The metastatic adenocarcinoma to a lymph node of the head and neck is specifically associated with the morphological description “سرطانة غدية نقيلية,” while the metastatic carcinoma to a lymph node of the head and neck corresponds to the morphological term “السرطان النقيلي.” These relationships underscore that each of these metastatic lymph‑node cancers is both a secondary malignancy and has a distinct histopathological identity expressed in Arabic terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 265
retrieved_docs.content: is an inverse‑ISA of نقائل إلى الرقبة and is also mapped to secondary malignancies. Several metastatic neoplasms involving lymph nodes of the head and neck—metastatic adenocarcinoma to lymph node of head and neck, metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck, and metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of head and neck—are all mapped to secondary malignancies. The adenocarcinoma variant is associated with the morphology metastatic glandular carcinoma (سرطانة غدية نقيلية), the carcinoma variant with metastatic cancer (السرطان النقيلي), and the squamous cell carcinoma variant with metastatic squamous cell carcinoma (سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية), a morphological form of squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck (سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق) that emphasizes spread to cervical lymph nodes and informs staging and therapeutic decisions. Malignant skin cancer (سرطان الجلد الخبيث) is classified as a secondary malignant tumor in other specified sites and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1154
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is classified as a type of الورم السرطاني (malignant tumor) and its associated morphology is described by الورم النقيلي (metastatic cancer). This indicates that the spread of carcinoma to the lymph nodes in the head and neck region represents a malignant neoplasm whose pathological features correspond to metastatic disease, a distinction that is important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2183
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of head and neck represents a specific manifestation of سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق (squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck). This relationship indicates that the metastatic disease involving cervical lymph nodes is a subset of the broader category of head‑and‑neck squamous cell carcinoma, emphasizing the importance of evaluating nodal involvement for accurate staging, prognosis, and treatment planning in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2667
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis to head and neck lymph node is recognized as a manifestation of head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق). This relationship indicates that the spread of malignant cells to the lymph nodes within the head and neck region is a specific form of head and neck cancer, highlighting its relevance for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic decision‑making.
